topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notice, stark white against the beige wall of the student union, felt like a physical blow. Liam stared at the printed letter, the ink blurring slightly under his gaze. *Closure*. The word echoed in the cavernous, suddenly silent hallway, each syllable a tiny shard of something shattering inside him. He clutched the paper so tightly his knuckles were white, the cheap paper crinkling under the pressure. He kept glancing up at the bustling students milling about, their laughter sounding impossibly bright and distant. A cold, clammy feeling began to creep up his neck, and he instinctively pulled his hoodie tighter around his chest, as if the fabric could somehow shield him from the crushing weight of the news. He took a shaky breath, the air tasting thin and metallic in his mouth, and slowly, painfully, folded the document, tucking it deep into his backpack, desperate to make the reality shrink back into the paper.

[story 2]

"So, we're done?" Maya whispered, the sound barely audible over the low hum of the campus coffee machine. She couldn't look at the administrative bulletin board; the official announcement detailing the campus shutdown seemed to vibrate with an awful finality. Her hands started to tremble uncontrollably, a fine vibration that shot up her arms and into her shoulders. She felt a sudden, intense urge to flee, to bolt out of the building and disappear into the indifferent city streets. She fiddled with the strap of her messenger bag, her movements jerky and uncoordinated. When she finally managed to look at the notice, the lines swam. A hot flush rose from her chest, spreading outwards, making her skin feel too tight, too exposed. She mumbled something incoherent, turning her face toward the window, watching the familiar quad, suddenly feeling like an intruder in a place that was actively erasing itself.

[story 3]

I kept re-reading the email, my eyes scanning the same paragraph over and over. Each time I read the sentence confirming the closure, a strange tightness seized my throat. I pressed the heels of my hands against my eyes, trying to physically push the information away, to force my brain to process something else. My jaw locked so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. The fluorescent lights of the library seemed too harsh, illuminating every speck of dust, every forgotten textbook, emphasizing the emptiness that was rapidly descending upon this place. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the floor, and began pacing the narrow aisle. My breath hitched in ragged gasps, and I ran a hand through my hair, feeling utterly exposed, as if every student in the building was watching this display of utter defeat.
